title: AI-Powered Infrastructure Monitoring from Municipal Dashcam Fleets
teaser: 75+ hours of geo-tagged dashcam footage captured, enriched with automated bounding box detection across 10 infrastructure defect categories, and deployed as the training foundation for a real-time model that flags potholes, cracked roads, and damaged signs from routine municipal patrols.
headlineStatLabel: Hours of street-level footage enriched with AI detection
summary: Built the training data pipeline for a Midwest county government deploying AI-powered infrastructure monitoring across its municipal vehicle fleet. Captured 75+ hours of geo-tagged dashcam footage across hundreds of route-miles, then enriched every clip through a hybrid annotation pipeline — automated vision model detection validated and corrected by expert human annotators — covering 10 defect categories including cracked road surfaces, potholes, faded lane markings, and damaged signs. The enriched dataset now powers a model that runs inference on live feeds from garbage trucks, utility vans, and patrol cars — generating maintenance tickets automatically as vehicles drive their normal routes.
challenge: The county's public works department relied on manual inspection to track infrastructure deterioration — inspectors drove periodic routes and logged issues by hand, creating a maintenance backlog that was perpetually weeks behind reality. With over 200 lane-miles of roads, dozens of intersections, and thousands of signs to monitor, the traditional approach couldn't keep pace. The county wanted to mount dashcams on existing municipal vehicles and use computer vision to flag issues automatically from the video feed. But that required training data: tens of hours of real local footage with labeled infrastructure defects, captured under the specific conditions those vehicles encounter — wet roads, overcast skies, night patrols, residential streets alongside commercial corridors.
approach:
We deployed a fleet of vehicle-mounted dashcams across the county over a focused collection campaign, capturing 75+ hours of street-level footage covering every major road, intersection, and residential block. Routes were planned for geographic completeness — ensuring diverse conditions including residential neighborhoods, commercial corridors, school zones, and industrial areas under varying weather and lighting.

Annotation combined automated AI enrichment with expert human review. First, a vision model analyzed representative frames from each clip to generate structured infrastructure condition reports and bounding box detections across 10 defect categories. Human annotators then validated and corrected every detection — verifying bounding box accuracy, reclassifying edge cases (a patched crack vs. active deterioration), and adding defects the model missed. This hybrid approach delivered the precision of human judgment at the speed of automated processing.

The detection taxonomy was co-developed with the county's public works team to match their existing maintenance categorization system — ensuring that model outputs map directly to work order types. Each clip's final annotation combined machine-generated bounding boxes, human-verified labels, natural language scene descriptions, and structured metadata — creating training pairs rich enough for the county's model to learn from.

The hybrid pipeline processed the full 75+ hour corpus in days rather than the months that purely manual annotation would have required, while maintaining the accuracy standard that safety-critical infrastructure monitoring demands.

results.label: Total video data collected and enriched

impact: The enriched dataset became the training foundation for the county's automated infrastructure monitoring system. Municipal vehicles now capture footage as part of their normal routes — garbage trucks, utility vans, and patrol cars all contribute to a continuous street-level survey without any additional cost or staffing. The model flags defects in near real-time, automatically generating maintenance tickets categorized by severity and location. In the first month of deployment, the system identified 3x more infrastructure defects than the previous manual inspection process — the majority being cracked road surfaces and faded lane markings that would have gone unnoticed until they became safety hazards. The county estimates the automated system covers every road segment at least twice per week, compared to the previous quarterly manual inspection cycle.
whyItMatters: Every municipality faces the same problem: infrastructure deteriorates faster than manual inspection can track it. Sending inspectors on periodic routes creates a permanently stale picture of road conditions — by the time an issue is logged, it may have already caused an accident or become far more expensive to repair. By layering AI inference on dashcam footage from vehicles already driving every street, cities get a continuously updated infrastructure health map at near-zero marginal cost. The critical enabler is high-quality, locally-specific training data — the same streets, weather patterns, and deterioration types the model will encounter in production. This engagement demonstrated that a focused collection campaign, enriched with automated vision analysis, can produce a production-ready training dataset for municipal AI deployment in days, not months.

faqs.question: What types of infrastructure issues does the system detect?
faqs.answer: Ten categories, co-developed with the county's public works department: cracked road surfaces, potholes, faded lane markings, damaged or missing signs, broken sidewalks, overgrown vegetation, litter and illegal dumping, damaged utility poles, deteriorated building facades, and general road surface degradation. Each detection includes pixel-accurate bounding box coordinates and a severity classification.

faqs.question: How does the detection pipeline work?
faqs.answer: Each video frame is processed by a vision model with a structured detection prompt specifying the 10 infrastructure categories. The model returns bounding boxes with normalized coordinates and labels. For the training dataset, representative frames were processed per clip. In production, the system runs inference at 1 frame per second on live feeds from the vehicle fleet.

faqs.question: How accurate is zero-shot detection?
faqs.answer: Zero-shot detection on infrastructure categories is comparable to purpose-built object detection models without any training. The primary value of the locally-collected dataset is enabling fine-tuning on region-specific conditions, which significantly improves precision for deterioration patterns unique to the area — such as specific cracking patterns caused by local climate and soil conditions.

faqs.question: How quickly can this pipeline be deployed for a new municipality?
faqs.answer: The collection and enrichment pipeline is fully reusable. A new municipality needs a focused 1-2 week dashcam collection campaign to capture their specific road conditions, followed by automated enrichment that processes the entire corpus in hours. The detection taxonomy is configurable — different regions have different priorities based on climate, terrain, and infrastructure age.

faqs.question: What happens after a defect is detected?
faqs.answer: Each detection is geo-tagged with GPS coordinates and timestamped, then routed into the county's existing maintenance management system as a categorized work order. Priority scoring considers defect type, severity, location (school zones and major intersections are weighted higher), and historical frequency. The public works team reviews flagged items on a dashboard rather than driving inspection routes.
